claims.claim: The policy's sole 'not medically necessary' provision applies only to acute low back pain under six weeks without red flags.
The member has chronic low back pain with onset January 1, 2016, and therefore falls outside the acute-pain exclusion.
Policy Criterion 2.1 deems lumbar MRI medically necessary for pain ≥6 weeks that failed a conservative therapy trial (PT, NSAID/analgesic, or supervised home exercise).
The member's pain has persisted since 2016, far exceeding the six-week duration element of Criterion 2.1.
The member has documented failed conservative therapy via NSAID (Ibuprofen 400 MG, ordered 3/18/2026, active) and prior Acetaminophen 325 MG.
Persistence of pain since 2016 despite analgesic/NSAID management demonstrates the conservative trial failed.
The record does not document red-flags (2.2), radicular neurologic findings (2.3), or surgical planning (2.4); those grounds are not asserted.
The appeal may be filed in writing within 180 days of the denial notice and is timely.
A peer-to-peer review with a plan medical director may be requested within 14 days.

missing_elements: Member date of birth is null and absent from the letter — a common payer identity-match field.
No actual date of service is stated; the letter only lists 'Service Date CPT: 72148' with no service date, and denial parse lacks it.
No ICD-10 diagnosis codes are present (icd10_codes empty), which typically supports a medical-necessity argument tying the chronic LBP diagnosis to imaging.
No individualized wet/typed signature or credential/contact block; letter is signed only 'Patient Access Team' rather than by/attested by the ordering physician.
Auth reference is null (acceptable for a post-service claim, but note explicitly).

needs_human.issue: All clinical facts driving Grounds 1 and 2 (2016 onset/chronicity, Ibuprofen order, Acetaminophen use) are NOT in the enforced-citation evidence list — only the policy is cited. These clinical assertions must be verified against and cited to the actual medical record before sending, or the payer can dismiss them as unsupported.

The 'failed conservative therapy' argument is weak: a still-active Ibuprofen order dated 3/18/2026 reads as an ongoing/recent trial, not a documented failed trial. Confirm the chart actually documents a completed conservative trial with an inadequate response before relying on Criterion 2.1.

Denial date (2026-07-18), paid date (2026-07-18), and intended filing date (2026-07-18) are all identical — verify the denial was actually received/finalized and the filing date is correct; the same-day pattern is anomalous.
Add member DOB, actual date of service, and ICD-10 diagnosis code(s) to strengthen identity match and the medical-necessity linkage.
Obtain a proper physician attestation/signature; a self-styled 'Patient Access Team' signature may be insufficient for a physician medical-necessity appeal.

summary: The appeal is timely (90 days remaining before the 10/16/2026 deadline) and its policy-based framing is strong — the acute-pain exclusion clearly doesn't apply and Criterion 2.1's duration element is well-argued. However, every clinical fact underpinning the argument (2016 onset, NSAID/acetaminophen use) is uncited to the medical record, and the 'failed conservative therapy' claim is weak because an active, recent Ibuprofen order does not by itself document a failed trial. A human must verify and cite the clinical record, shore up the conservative-therapy failure, and add missing identifiers/signature before submission.
